blocks.content: Spark Structured Streaming treats an incoming data stream as an unbounded table — new rows are continuously appended. Queries are written identically to batch DataFrame queries; Spark handles the incremental execution automatically. This unification of batch and streaming is Spark's biggest advantage over older systems like Storm or Flink's low-level API.

rows: State store tracks aggregation state across micro-batches

blocks.content: The trigger controls when Spark checks for new data and processes it. Choosing the wrong trigger is a common source of latency and cost issues.

from pyspark.sql.streaming import DataStreamWriter

# Default: start next micro-batch as soon as previous one finishes
query = df.writeStream.trigger()

# Fixed interval: process every N seconds/minutes
query = df.writeStream.trigger(processingTime='30 seconds')

# Once: process all available data then stop (batch-style backfill)
query = df.writeStream.trigger(once=True)

# availableNow (Spark 3.3+): like Once but uses multiple micro-batches
# Better for large backlogs — processes all available data incrementally
query = df.writeStream.trigger(availableNow=True)

# Continuous processing (experimental): ~1ms latency, limited operations
query = df.writeStream.trigger(continuous='1 second')

# Start the query
query = df.writeStream \
    .trigger(processingTime='1 minute') \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', 's3://bucket/checkpoints/my_query') \
    .start('s3://bucket/output/')

rows: Only NEW rows that were produced in this micro-batch
Simple transformations, writes to append-only sinks (Kafka, files)
Only CHANGED rows in the result table since the last batch
Aggregations — only write updated aggregation values
Entire result table in every micro-batch

blocks.content: Aggregations with append mode require a watermark — Spark needs to know when a window is closed before appending results. Use update or complete mode for aggregations without watermarks.

Checkpoints record two things: (1) the offsets of the last-processed message in each source partition, and (2) the serialized state store (for stateful aggregations). On restart after a failure, Spark replays from the last checkpoint — enabling exactly-once end-to-end semantics.

# Checkpoint location is REQUIRED for production streaming queries
# Must be a fault-tolerant location (HDFS, S3, ADLS — NOT local disk)

query = df.writeStream \
    .outputMode('append') \
    .format('delta') \
    .option('checkpointLocation', 's3://bucket/checkpoints/events_streaming') \
    .start('s3://bucket/output/events')

# Monitor the query
print(query.status)    # {'message': 'Processing new data', 'isDataAvailable': True, ...}
print(query.lastProgress)  # Rich metrics: batchId, inputRowsPerSecond, processedRows...

# Wait for termination (blocks until query stops or fails)
query.awaitTermination()

# Stop gracefully
query.stop()

Exactly-once guarantee: Requires both (1) a replayable source (Kafka tracks offsets, files track which files were read) and (2) an idempotent sink (Delta Lake writes with checkpoints). Kafka → Spark → Delta is the gold standard exactly-once pipeline.

rune_cards.definition: Structured Streaming's default processing model. Collects new records since the last batch into a mini-DataFrame and processes it using the same Spark SQL engine. Latency typically 100ms–30s depending on trigger. Easier to reason about than continuous processing.

Persistent record of (1) source offsets read so far and (2) state store snapshot. Stored in a fault-tolerant location (S3/HDFS). Enables Spark to restart exactly where it left off after a failure. Required for exactly-once semantics.

Controls which rows are written to the sink in each micro-batch. append: only new rows. update: only changed rows. complete: full result table. append is most common; complete is for small result tables (leaderboards, counts).

Trigger mode (Spark 3.3+) that processes all data available at query start time, then stops — like a batch job but using streaming semantics (checkpoints, state). Better than trigger(once=True) for large backlogs.

quiz.question: You have a streaming query computing windowed counts: groupBy(window('ts', '5 minutes')).count(). When a window closes, you want to write final counts once and never update them. Which output mode?
options: complete — writes all windows every batch
update — writes changed windows every batch
append — writes closed windows once (requires watermark)
overwrite — replaces the sink every batch
quiz.feedback: append mode writes each result row exactly once — when Spark determines the window is closed and will not receive late data. This requires a watermark to define when a window is 'closed'. Without a watermark Spark can never be sure no late data is coming, so append mode with aggregations requires a watermark.

quiz.question: Your streaming job fails. When it restarts, it reads from the checkpoint. Which guarantee does this provide?
options: At-most-once: some messages may be skipped
At-least-once: some messages may be processed twice
Exactly-once: each message processed exactly once end-to-end
quiz.feedback: With a replayable source (Kafka) and an idempotent sink (Delta Lake), Spark's checkpoint provides exactly-once semantics. Spark replays from the last committed checkpoint offset, and Delta's transaction log prevents duplicate writes even if the same data arrives twice.
